title: Lang2MLIP: End-to-End Language-to-Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Development with Autonomous Agentic Workflows
authors: Wenwen Li, Yuki Orimo, Nontawat Charoenphakdee
abstract: Developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for complex materials systems remains challenging because it requires expertise in atomistic simulations, machine learning, and workflow design, as well as iterative active learning procedures. Existing automated pipelines typically assume a fixed sequence of stages or depend on domain experts, which limits their adaptability to heterogeneous materials systems where the optimal curriculum is not known in advance. To lower the barrier to developing MLIPs for non-experts, we propose Lang2MLIP, a multi-agent framework that takes natural-language input and formulates end-to-end MLIP development as a sequential decision-making problem solved by large language models (LLMs). At each step, a decision-making agent observes the current dataset, model, evaluation results, and execution log, and then automatically selects an appropriate action to improve the model. This removes the need for a predefined pipeline and enables the agent to self-correct by revisiting earlier subsystems when new failures arise. We evaluate this approach on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces. These results suggest that LLM-based multi-agent systems are a promising direction for automating MLIP development and making it more accessible to non-experts.
summary: This paper introduces Lang2MLIP, an autonomous multi-agent framework that uses Large Language Models to automate the end-to-end development of machine learning interatomic potentials. By treating potential development as a sequential decision-making task, the system can independently navigate complex training curricula for heterogeneous materials like battery interfaces. This reduces the need for human expertise in designing active learning pipelines.
main_problem: Developing robust Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) for complex, heterogeneous materials like the Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) is difficult due to the need for expert-driven, iterative active learning workflows.
main_result: The Lang2MLIP framework successfully autonomously developed a stable and accurate MLIP for a multi-layer SEI system, achieving high fidelity to reference PFP data through an emergent, self-correcting training curriculum.
method: A multi-agent LLM-driven framework (using Claude Opus) that treats MLIP development as a sequential decision-making problem, automating data sampling, training, and model refinement.
model_or_system: The physical system is a complex, four-layer Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) in lithium batteries, comprising graphite, amorphous Li2CO3, amorphous LEDC, and a liquid electrolyte. The model is a Moment Tensor Potential (MTP) trained via an agentic workflow.
key_observables: Density, Radial Distribution Functions (RDF), Mean Squared Displacement (MSD), energy per atom, and force Mean Absolute Error (MAE).
important_parameters: Temperature (300K to 2000K), sampling ratios, and training epochs.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that workflow-level coordination via an LLM is sufficient for autonomous development and that underlying simulation tools are properly exposed to the agents.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides an overview of the Lang2MLIP framework phases; Figure 2 shows a schematic of the hierarchical four-layer SEI system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MLIP development, describes the Lang2MLIP multi-agent architecture, details the application to the SEI system, presents ablation studies comparing manual vs. autonomous workflows, and concludes with performance validation against reference data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
